subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: Exact contents listed in the provided rulebook: 110 cards, 7 easels, 7 erasable felt markers, and 1 rulebook. For a standard game, you will create a temporary 13-card deck from the 110 cards; the rest are returned to the box and not used in that session.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Shuffle all cards and randomly draw 13 to form a face-down deck in the middle of the table. Give each player one easel and one erasable felt marker. Ensure all players can write privately and then reveal clues to each other during the comparison phase. In a 3-player game variant, each player takes two easels because each player writes two clues per turn.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: Randomly choose the first active player (the guesser for that turn). The active player draws the top card without looking and places it into their easel support so all other players can see the five candidate words. The active player then chooses a number from 1 to 5 to indicate which mystery word clue-givers must reference. If clue-givers do not know that selected word, they can ask the active player to choose a different number before clue writing begins.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Each turn follows 4 phases, then a role pass:
1. Choose the Mystery Word: Active player displays top card and selects word number (1-5).
2. Clue Selection: Every non-active player secretly writes one single-word clue without communication.
3. Comparing Clues: Active player closes eyes; clue-givers reveal to each other and cancel all identical or invalid clues.
4. Guess: Active player opens eyes and makes exactly one guess based only on remaining clues.
5. End of Turn: Resolve success/failure/skip effects, rotate active player to the left, erase old clues, and continue.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Clue writing rules:
- Each clue must be one word.
- Digits, numbers, acronyms, onomatopoeia, and special characters count as words per rulebook examples.
- No communication during clue writing.

Clue cancellation rules in comparison phase:
- Identical clues are cancelled (both/all copies).
- Word-family variants are treated as identical and cancelled (e.g., plural/gender/spelling variants).
- Invalid clues are cancelled, including: the mystery word spelled differently, the mystery word in another language, a same-family derivative of the mystery word, invented words, and homophones judged invalid by rule examples.
- Cancelled clues are hidden by tipping easels down.

Guess resolution:
- Active player gets only one guess.
- Correct guess: place that card face up in success area.
- Wrong guess: return current card and also return the top card of the deck to the box.
- Skip: if active player chooses not to answer, return only the current card to the box.
- If all clues were cancelled, return current card to the box and move directly to end of turn (no guess made).

Pronunciation note from rules:
- If the active player guesses a word with the same pronunciation as the mystery word, that guess is valid.

Final-turn penalty note:
- If the active player gives a wrong guess during the final turn, also remove one previously successful card.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
This is a fully cooperative hidden-writing system with simultaneous clue generation and mandatory duplicate pruning. The game’s core tension is not only finding a useful clue, but finding a useful clue that no one else will also write.

3-player variant (explicitly in provided text): each player takes two easels and writes two clues during clue selection. Duplicate cancellation still applies across all written clues. This variant restores clue volume and uncertainty at low player count.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
End trigger: The game ends when the 13-card deck is empty.

Scoring: Count successfully guessed cards and compare to the official rating table:
- 13: Perfect score!
- 12: Incredible!
- 11: Awesome!
- 9-10: Wow, not bad at all!
- 7-8: Average.
- 4-6: Good start.
- 0-3: Try again.

All score sources in this game:
- +1 successful card for each correctly guessed mystery word (tracked by face-up success cards).
- No other positive score sources exist.

Negative outcome effects that reduce final total:
- Wrong guess removes current card plus one extra top-deck card from potential scoring.
- Final-turn wrong guess removes an additional previously successful card.
- Skip and all-cancelled-clue turns remove current card from potential scoring.

Tie-breakers: Not applicable in standard play, because the team shares one cooperative score rather than competing individual totals.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content: In the first few turns, prioritize clue diversity over maximum precision. Before writing, mentally list the two most obvious clues and avoid them; obvious clues collide and disappear. Use one-step-removed associations (category, context, function, opposite, famous example) that are still clear to the guesser but less likely to duplicate. As active player, pick a word number you believe most clue-givers will understand immediately, because confusion at word selection can waste the turn before clue quality even matters.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Writing the most obvious synonym every time. It often gets cancelled and leaves the guesser with weak leftovers.
2) Using invalid clue forms (same family, disguised target word, translated target) and losing clue value for free.
3) Trying to be so clever that clues become opaque. A unique clue is worthless if it cannot guide the guesser.
4) Ignoring table meta: if your group repeatedly uses certain clue types, collisions become predictable unless you adapt.
5) Guessing too quickly as active player. Taking a short moment to connect all surviving clues usually increases accuracy, especially after heavy clue cancellation.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
1) Safety vs uniqueness: Do you write a very clear clue that might duplicate, or a more original clue that might survive but be weaker?
2) Specificity vs breadth: Narrow clues can lock onto the right word but risk invalid overlap; broader clues survive more often but may not differentiate enough.
3) Risk management near end of deck: In late turns, decide whether the active player should attempt a difficult guess or skip to avoid larger penalties from a wrong answer, especially with the final-turn penalty in mind.

metadata.notes: Primary source: Just One rulebook text provided in prompt, including setup, 4-phase turn flow, clue validity/cancellation rules, score table, and 3-player variant.
